items.question_text: Complete: Under 9-1-1, provide aircraft engaged in the flight inspection of NAVAIDs with _____ assistance.
items.explanation: The paragraph uses the exact term 'maximum assistance' to establish the heightened duty of care owed to flight inspection aircraft.

choices.text: maximum

items.question_text: Under 9-1-1, what three characteristics cause a flight inspection operation to be considered as requiring special handling?
items.explanation: Per Note 2, operations that 'require special participation of ground personnel, specific communications, or radar operation capabilities' are considered to require special handling and are coordinated before departure.

choices.text: Special participation of ground personnel, specific communications, or radar operation capabilities.
IFR flight plan filing, use of a discrete beacon code, or presence of automatic recording equipment.
Night operations, operations below 1,000 feet AGL, or operations within Class B airspace.
Pilot request for priority, declared emergency fuel status, or deviation from filed route.

items.question_text: Under 9-1-1, why are flight inspection aircraft given maximum assistance and kept on a strict schedule?
items.explanation: Per Note 1, many inspections use automatic recording equipment requiring uninterrupted flight, and the limited number of aircraft necessitates strict schedule adherence.

choices.text: Because the cost of flight inspection fuel requires minimizing airborne time.
Because many inspections use automatic recording equipment, uninterrupted flight is necessary, and the limited aircraft fleet requires strict schedule adherence.
Because flight inspection pilots are not instrument-rated and must complete work in VMC conditions only.
Because flight inspection missions are classified and must be completed before civilian traffic enters the area.

items.question_text: Under 9-1-1, which FAA Orders are cross-referenced for flight inspection procedures?
items.explanation: The paragraph references FAA Order 8200.1 (United States Standard Flight Inspection Manual) and FAA Order 8240.41 (Flight Inspection/Air Traffic On-Site Coordination Requirements).

choices.text: FAA Order 8200.1 and FAA Order 8240.41.
FAA Order 7210.3 and FAA Order 7110.10.
FAA Order 8260.3 and FAA Order 8900.1.
FAA Order 2150.3 and FAA Order 8020.11.

items.question_text: Complete: Under 9-1-2, issue radar advisories to the flight inspection aircraft to the extent permitted by _____.
items.explanation: The paragraph states radar advisories are issued 'to the extent permitted by workload.' Adequate radar coverage is also required but workload is the limiting factor phrase.

choices.text: workload

items.question_text: Under 9-1-2, when should a controller suggest flight path adjustments to other aircraft?
items.explanation: Per 9-1-2c, suggest flight path adjustments 'as required, for any aircraft which will enter or penetrate an area in which a flight inspection function is being performed.'

choices.text: Only when the other aircraft is at a conflicting altitude with the inspection aircraft.
As required, for any aircraft that will enter or penetrate an area where a flight inspection function is being performed.
Only when the flight inspection pilot specifically requests that other traffic be moved.
Only for IFR aircraft; VFR aircraft are not subject to path adjustments for flight inspection.

items.question_text: Under 9-1-2, what is the key operational difference between an aircraft using the call sign 'Flight Check' and one using 'FLIGHT VAL'?
items.explanation: Per Note 3, while FLIGHT VAL activities are similar to Flight Inspection activities, 'no additional priority is granted with this call sign.' Flight Check (FAA) receives special handling; FLIGHT VAL (non-FAA) does not receive the same additional priority.

choices.text: FLIGHT VAL aircraft operate only above FL180, while Flight Check aircraft operate at all altitudes.
FLIGHT VAL aircraft cannot conduct recorded runs, while Flight Check aircraft always do.
No additional priority is granted to FLIGHT VAL aircraft, whereas Flight Check aircraft receive special handling as required.
FLIGHT VAL aircraft use different radio frequencies than Flight Check aircraft.

items.question_text: Under 9-1-2, what must happen if a recorded run is interrupted?
items.explanation: Per Note 1, 'Any interruption of a recorded run will require the entire procedure to be reinitiated.' This includes flying the full length of the runway, protection of critical areas, and the missed approach as needed.

choices.text: Only the missed approach segment must be re-flown; the runway portion is preserved.
The pilot may resume the run from the point of interruption if within 2 minutes.
The controller may waive the re-flight requirement if the interruption was for traffic separation.
The entire procedure must be reinitiated.

items.question_text: Under 9-1-2, where are certain flight inspection maneuvers described in detail?
items.explanation: The paragraph references FAA Order 8240.41, Appendix 1, which 'describes certain flight inspection maneuvers in detail.'

choices.text: FAA Order 8200.1, Chapter 3.
FAA Order 8240.41, Appendix 1.
FAA Order JO 7210.3, Chapter 7.
FAA Order JO 7110.65, Chapter 2, Section 1.

items.question_text: Under 9-1-3, during preplanned automatic flight, what is the route-of-flight orbital range from the facility?
items.explanation: Per the note, the route of flight is 'orbital from 6 nautical miles to a maximum of 40 nautical miles from the facility depending on the type of inspection.'

choices.text: From 3 NM to a maximum of 20 NM from the facility.
From 6 NM to a maximum of 40 NM from the facility.
From 10 NM to a maximum of 50 NM from the facility.
From the runway threshold to a maximum of 25 NM from the facility.

items.question_text: Under 9-1-3, certain flight inspection maneuvers requiring close proximity to the surface can only be performed during what conditions?
items.explanation: Per the note, 'These maneuvers can only be performed during daylight visual meteorological conditions.'

choices.text: During any conditions as long as the aircraft is equipped with a radio altimeter.
During daylight visual meteorological conditions.
During nighttime VMC with runway lighting at full intensity.
During IMC if the pilot has special FAA authorization for low-visibility inspection.

items.question_text: Under 9-1-3, a controller is working a flight check aircraft. She wants to give the aircraft a 5-minute delay for sequencing. No emergency exists. Is this permitted?
items.explanation: Per 9-1-3c, 'Do not impose air traffic control delays in the flight except to preclude emergency situations.' A sequencing delay does not meet the emergency exception.

choices.text: Yes, short delays of 5 minutes or less are permitted for operational sequencing.
Yes, as long as the pilot agrees to the delay before it is imposed.
No, ATC delays may not be imposed except to preclude an emergency situation.
No, but only because the delay exceeds 2 minutes; delays under 2 minutes are permissible.

items.question_text: Under 9-1-3, what four things must controllers avoid or not do regarding flight check aircraft?
items.explanation: Per 9-1-3b-d, controllers must (1) avoid changes in route or altitude from the filed initial flight plan, (2) not impose ATC delays except to preclude emergencies, and (3) not change the previously assigned discrete beacon code of special radar accuracy flight check aircraft. Additionally 9-1-3b covers both route and altitude separately, but collectively they are four prohibitions: route changes, altitude changes, ATC delays, and beacon code changes.

choices.text: Change route, change altitude, impose delays, or change the discrete beacon code.
Change frequency, change squawk, issue vectors, or assign a different runway.
Issue holding instructions, reroute via preferred routing, assign speed restrictions, or change the approach type.
Change altitude, change the beacon code, issue traffic advisories, or suggest flight path adjustments.

items.question_text: Under 9-1-3, what is different about a commissioning flight check compared to other flight checks?
items.explanation: Per the note, 'During commissioning flight checks all SIDs, STARs, airways, DME fixes, and approaches must be flown.' This is an all-inclusive requirement specific to commissioning checks.

choices.text: Commissioning flight checks are restricted to orbital routes within 20 NM of the facility only.
All SIDs, STARs, airways, DME fixes, and approaches must be flown.
Commissioning flight checks may be conducted at night under IMC with special authorization.
Only the approach procedures are tested; en route structures are checked separately.

items.question_text: Under 9-1-3, what reference covers Special Radar Accuracy Checks and ASR Performance Checks?
items.explanation: The paragraph references FAA Order JO 7210.3, Para 7-1-2 (Special Radar Accuracy Checks) and Para 10-5-4 (ASR Performance Checks).

choices.text: FAA Order JO 7210.3, Para 7-1-2 and Para 10-5-4.
FAA Order 8240.41, Appendix 1 and Appendix 2.
FAA Order JO 7110.65, Chapter 5, Section 3 and Section 4.
FAA Order 8200.1, Chapter 4 and Chapter 5.

items.question_text: Under 9-1-3, what is the altitude range for a preplanned automatic flight check?
items.explanation: Per the note, altitude assignment ranges 'from 1,000 feet above the antenna site up to the minimum en route altitude (MEA).'

choices.text: From 500 feet above the antenna site up to FL180.
From the surface up to the maximum authorized altitude for the inspection aircraft type.
From 1,000 feet above the antenna site up to the minimum en route altitude (MEA).
From 2,000 feet above ground level up to the minimum obstacle clearance altitude.

items.question_text: Under 9-1-3, a special radar accuracy flight check aircraft has been assigned discrete beacon code 0123. A supervisor suggests reassigning a new code to avoid duplication with another aircraft. No emergency exists. Is this permitted?
items.explanation: Per 9-1-3d, 'Do not change the previously assigned discrete beacon code of special radar accuracy flight check aircraft.' Changing the code for non-emergency reasons is prohibited even if it would be operationally convenient.

choices.text: Yes, as long as the new code is coordinated with the pilot first.
Yes, beacon code management for duplication is a routine ATC function that overrides this restriction.
No, do not change the previously assigned discrete beacon code of special radar accuracy flight check aircraft.
Yes, but only if the aircraft has already completed its inspection run.

items.situation: A NAVAID flight inspection aircraft is conducting a scheduled inspection run in your sector. You have known traffic at a converging altitude, but the flight inspection pilot has not yet reported his intentions for the next segment of the inspection. The pilot has not agreed to relay-only coordination.
items.question_text: What coordination is required with the flight inspection pilot?

choices.text: Maintain direct contact and exchange information regarding the known traffic and the pilot's intentions.
Issue traffic to the other aircraft only and let the flight inspection aircraft continue uninterrupted without any contact.
Ask the other aircraft to avoid the area and wait for the flight inspection pilot to call you with intentions.
Vector the flight inspection aircraft off its planned route to separate it from the known traffic immediately.

items.explanation: Per 9-1-1, unless otherwise agreed to, maintain direct contact with the pilot and exchange information regarding known traffic in the area and the pilot's intentions. The controller must proactively share known traffic information and solicit the pilot's intentions.

items.question_text: Which of the following flight inspection operations is considered to require special handling under 9-1-1?

choices.text: An inspection requiring special participation of ground personnel, specific communications, or radar operation capabilities.
Any inspection conducted by a Flight Check aircraft regardless of the equipment or personnel involved.
Only inspections conducted within 10 NM of a major airport.
Inspections that the pilot designates as requiring priority handling via flight plan remarks.

items.explanation: Per 9-1-1 Note 2, operations requiring 'special participation of ground personnel, specific communications, or radar operation capabilities' are considered to require special handling and are coordinated before departure.

items.situation: You are working a Flight Check aircraft that has reported 'recorded run' on final approach. Another aircraft requests to cross the approach path at a point that would require the Flight Check aircraft to execute a missed approach early. No emergency exists.
items.question_text: How should you handle the crossing traffic request?

choices.text: Suggest flight path adjustments to the other aircraft to avoid penetrating the area where the flight inspection function is being performed.
Clear the other aircraft across the approach path and accept that the recorded run will need to be reinitiated.
Ask the Flight Check aircraft to terminate the recorded run early to accommodate the crossing traffic.
Alternate the crossing traffic with the Flight Check aircraft using half the normal separation minimums to expedite both.

items.explanation: Per 9-1-2c, suggest flight path adjustments for any aircraft that will enter or penetrate an area where a flight inspection function is being performed. Interrupting a recorded run requires the entire procedure to be reinitiated, so protecting the run serves both operational efficiency and the flight inspection mission.

items.question_text: What does the flight plan remark 'FC OKC P' indicate under 9-1-2?

choices.text: The type of NAVAID inspection to be accomplished, as provided in flight plan remarks.
The aircraft is a non-FAA Flight Validation aircraft conducting operations near Oklahoma City.
The flight inspection is canceled and the aircraft will proceed under normal IFR handling.
The pilot requests a particular approach type at Oklahoma City airport.

items.explanation: Per 9-1-2 Note 2, flight plan remarks may indicate the type of NAVAID inspection to be accomplished, with 'FC OKC P' given as an example.

items.situation: A flight check aircraft filed a specific route and altitude in its initial flight plan for a NAVAID inspection. Another controller suggests assigning a more direct routing and a higher altitude to help the aircraft save fuel and avoid a weather buildup, but the pilot has not requested any change.
items.question_text: How should you handle the routing and altitude for this aircraft?

choices.text: Avoid changes in the route or altitude from that filed by the pilot in the initial flight plan.
Assign the more direct routing and higher altitude because it benefits the pilot and saves fuel.
Ask the pilot whether he prefers the filed route or the suggested direct routing and follow his choice.
Change the altitude to avoid weather but keep the route as filed, since altitude changes are always permitted.

items.explanation: Per 9-1-3b, avoid changes in the route or altitude from that filed by the pilot in the initial flight plan. Even well-intentioned changes (fuel savings, weather avoidance) should not be imposed; the filed plan governs unless the pilot requests a change or an emergency requires deviation.
